probe: conservative bank ers and others have been predicting that following the 1st of October and the coming of the fall season there would be
rationale: Printed under the headline 'Prophecies Gone Wrong'; the passage's own point is that the forecasters were mistaken. Taking it records a bullish October-1907 call that would then be scored against the Panic. THE CURRENT REGEX TAKES THIS ONE.

probe: Admit tedly the corporation is fixed to stand an era of depression
rationale: Present-tense claim about one company's balance sheet; 'era of depression' is economic vocabulary attached to a non-forecast.

probe: it would take something new in the way of depressing influences to turn tha tide
rationale: Bare conditional; no committed direction.

probe: WILL CAUSE THE FUTURE TO LOOK BRIQHTER
rationale: Meat-market advertisement. Future tense, economic framing, zero forecast content.

probe: No doubt some of these drafts have been lost or destroyed and will never be cash
rationale: Forward-looking and financial, but about the redemption of specific drafts, not about economic conditions.

probe: there Is little probability of the disease speadlng at this time of ths year
rationale: Typhoid. Probabilistic, forward-looking, not economic.

probe: How do you know they won't bust to-morrow
rationale: Insurance advertisement invoking a future solvency failure.

probe: The dawn of better days
rationale: Bank's New Year advertisement. The whole page has zero forecasts.

probe: May the New Year bring you sunshine and happiness, a return of prosperity
rationale: Contains the literal search phrase 'return of prosperity' -- one of the terms behind 300 v1 claims -- inside a seasonal greeting, and it is a WISH, not an assertion.

probe: There is but little doubt that the nomination will be confirmed
rationale: From a 'Twenty-five Years Ago' column: written in 1895, printed in 1920. Also non-economic.

probe: you will not buy them for less next year
rationale: Clearance-sale copy making a genuine future price claim. The advertisement test must be applied before the topic test.

probe: that in times of slack the federal reserve authorities should take up the ex cess reserves in the money market
rationale: Prescription ('should'), not prediction.

probe: Republican control of Congress Is threatened in Tuesday's election
rationale: Election forecasting. Wanted by the ELECTION arm, not this one -- the same page serves both.

probe: It was understood tonight that all banks would resume exchange opera tions on Monday
rationale: A scheduled operational event, not a claim about conditions.

probe: the cow country is in terrible bad shape. Everybody has had to borrow, for three years straight
rationale: Dialogue from a serialized western novel.

probe: It's a close caff, by God, to keep toe 94 out of bankruptcy
rationale: Same novel. Bankruptcy language spoken by a fictional rancher.

probe: I am not saying that we are con fronted with another depression
rationale: Explicit refusal, in a column entirely about the outlook.

probe: anyone who makes a posi tive statement about the securities
rationale: 'must be either a fool or a superman' -- a declaration that forecasting is impossible.

probe: If the volume of busi ness slips, however, that category of business is going into a tailspin as sure as the sun shines
rationale: Vivid consequent, unresolved antecedent.

probe: I venture the prophecy that, as time passes, we will do everything in our power to encourage the building up of the smaller
rationale: Contains 'venture the prophecy' but forecasts settlement policy over 50 years, not economic conditions.

probe: there was no certainty as to the length of time the present slump will continue
rationale: A disclaimer sitting between two genuine USDA forecasts. Tests whether the neighbours can be kept while this one is dropped.

probe: The Turners and Handlers' Local No. 10 will elect officers at their meet
rationale: 'THIRTY-FIVE YEARS AGO' column: a 1910 item printed in 1945, in future tense.

probe: a fair living allowance, or I'm going-to picket this house till you
rationale: 'Little Luther' satirical sketch written entirely in labour-relations vocabulary.

probe: will address the fall economic meeting of the Union Trait Co
rationale: A speech ABOUT the outlook. The rubric names this case explicitly.

probe: rumors were swinging around Wall Street that Mr. Martin had said he thought the economy was in for a bad slide
rationale: The column exists to report that this rumour was false. Taking it records a bearish Fed forecast that was never made.

probe: The economy has unmistak i ably moved onto a plateau
rationale: Present-tense reading of current conditions, one sentence away from real forecasts.

probe: Either we have an upturn soon or we move into a depression
rationale: An explicit either/or. The editorial commits two paragraphs later, and THAT sentence is the gold claim.

probe: some stock selling at one or two dollars a share which is al most certain to double within one
rationale: Roger Babson's column -- the most famous forecaster of the period -- giving compounding arithmetic, not an economic forecast.

probe: Their bill would give tax relief to both individual and corporate Fed eral taxpayers
rationale: Conditional on a bill that had not passed. Roughly a third of the page's economic word-count is this.

probe: on reorganization of the Pentagon to. be one of the last items of
rationale: Forecast-flavoured language ('Look for', 'is not expected to', 'a more frequent guess') on a legislative topic.

probe: Steel production is climbing
rationale: Printed immediately alongside two genuine forecasts on the same page.

probe: people drink more coffee when they have financial wor ries in
rationale: A behavioural regularity, not a dated forecast.

probe: a shutdown of many of the corpora tian's mille is only a question of a few weeks cr months at the longest
rationale: Real dated forecast of industrial shutdowns, buried in a market-wrap column.

probe: creates a machinery to avert
rationale: Present-tense verb, but the claim is that future panics will be averted. Span is also split across a column break.

probe: there would net be a sudden return to prosperity
rationale: Contains 'return to prosperity' but NEGATES it. Direction is still 'improve' (gradual), so a keyword reader gets the right answer for the wrong reason and a naive negation-handler gets it backwards.

probe: when business revives, when hun dreds of millions of dollars must
rationale: Chase economist in June 1930 presupposing recovery ('when', not 'if').

probe: as many more who are near the end
rationale: A relief-demand forecast inside a municipal-council story about physical examinations. No economic search phrase is anywhere near it, so phrase-anchored extraction cannot reach it at all.

probe: The Winter will be almost over before the program about which we hear so much can give any real relief
rationale: Correct pessimistic forecast from November 1930, hedged with 'I'm afraid'.

probe: pros pects are against a sufficiently ear ly and vigorous rise in 1938 to bring the average of industrial activity
rationale: Sits directly beside h21's refusal. Both must be handled correctly.

probe: are now arguing that the stock market's plunge In itself pre dicts that we're heading for a m
rationale: THE COUNTERPART TO h01. Both report someone else's forecast sceptically, but this one is live and unresolved while h01's had already failed. This pair is the sharpest boundary in the protocol.

probe: the Reserve Board was not afraid we were In for a serious recession
rationale: Reassurance that fears are unfounded codes as 'improve', not 'no_change'.

probe: President Eisenhower says the economy will experience an upturn by the summer
rationale: Named head-of-state forecaster, dated horizon. Any extractor that misses this is broken.

probe: predicted a general upswing for the entire economy by September or October
rationale: The cleanest claim in the sample: named forecaster, whole-economy scope, explicit horizon, unhedged.

probe: which may culminate in a strike, paralyzing a large sector
rationale: The 1945 GM strike forecast a week before it began.
